coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics are one of the compounds that slowly degrade through biological processes, which cannot be eliminated by conventional wastewater treatment techniques, usually due to their too large molecular weight. Nonetheless, there is scant study investigating the impact of an external magnetic field on the photocatalytic efficacy of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO, particularly for pharmaceutical degradation. The interplay between magnetic fields and photocatalysts is inadequately comprehended, and the long-term stability and scalability of these systems have not been sufficiently explored. This study sought to investigate the impact of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO as a catalyst on the magnetic field-assisted photocatalytic (photomagnetocatalytic, PMC) degradation of amoxicillin and ibuprofen in aqueous solutions. The physical and chemical properties of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst were analyzed using XRD, EDX, FESEM, TEM, XPS, XRD, PL, and DRS techniques. The research examined the effects of many parameters, including pH, initial pollutant concentration, catalyst powder concentration, and different voltages, on PMC degradation. The results demonstrated degradation efficiencies of 99 % for amoxicillin and 97 % for ibuprofen, accompanied by significant durability and reusability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics are one of the compounds that slowly degrade through biological processes, which cannot be eliminated by conventional wastewater treatment techniques, usually due to their too large molecular weight. Nonetheless, there is scant study investigating the impact of an external magnetic field on the photocatalytic efficacy of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO, particularly for pharmaceutical degradation. The interplay between magnetic fields and photocatalysts is inadequately comprehended, and the long-term stability and scalability of these systems have not been sufficiently explored. This study sought to investigate the impact of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO as a catalyst on the magnetic field-assisted photocatalytic (photomagnetocatalytic, PMC) degradation of amoxicillin and ibuprofen in aqueous solutions. The physical and chemical properties of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst were analyzed using XRD, EDX, FESEM, TEM, XPS, XRD, PL, and DRS techniques. The research examined the effects of many parameters, including pH, initial pollutant concentration, catalyst powder concentration, and different voltages, on PMC degradation. The results demonstrated degradation efficiencies of 99 % for amoxicillin and 97 % for ibuprofen, accompanied by significant durability and reusability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater refers to water that has been utilized and contaminated by various sources, including residential, industrial, commercial, and agricultural activities. This water is laden with diverse pollutants such as chemicals, organic substances, and pathogens, which can pose significant environmental and health risks if not adequately treated. Sources of wastewater encompass everyday household activities (such as bathing, cooking, and sewage disposal), industrial operations, and agricultural runoff. Effective wastewater treatment involves several stages designed to eliminate or neutralize contaminants, ensuring that the resulting water is safe for environmental release or reuse in various applications [1–4]. A photomagnetocatalytic (PMC) degradation uses a photocatalyst with magnetic properties typically by integrating magnetic materials such as iron oxide with photocatalytic materials such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) and zinc oxide (ZnO). This combination enables the catalyst to be easily separated from a reaction mixture using an external magnetic field, simplifying the recovery and reuse process [5,6]. Antibiotics are commonly used to treat bacterial infections in both humans and animals. They are also added to animal feed to enhance growth [7–11]. Consequently, large amounts of these drugs end up in water sources, especially when they are overused, resulting in antibiotic residues found in the environment known to pose a risk to ecosystems and public health [12,13]. Amoxicillin in wastewater can have significant environmental and health impacts. As a widely used antibiotic, amoxicillin often passes through human and animal bodies unmetabolized and enters wastewater systems. Conventional treatment processes may not effectively remove this compound, leading to its release into natural water bodies. In the environment, amoxicillin can disrupt aquatic ecosystems by promoting the development of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, which can spread to other organisms and potentially reach humans, diminishing the efficacy of antibiotic treatments. Furthermore, its presence can negatively affect aquatic life, altering microbial communities and impacting the health and biodiversity of ecosystems [14–17]. Ibuprofen, a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), is widely employed for pain relief, fever reduction, and inflammation alleviation. It is readily available without a prescription. It can effectively treat various conditions such as headaches, muscle pain, arthritis, menstrual discomfort, and minor injuries [18,19]. However, the persistence of ibuprofen in the environment can result in its accumulation in the food chain, posing risks to wildlife and potentially humans. Therefore, it is crucial to efficiently degrade ibuprofen in wastewater to mitigate these impacts, safeguard ecosystems, and ensure the safety of water resources for human consumption and other uses [20,21]. Ti3C2 MXene is a 2D material from the MXene family consisting of transition metal carbides, nitrides, or carbonitrides. It has a distinctive layered structure and high electrical conductivity, coupled with a large surface area, exceptional mechanical properties, and adjustable surface chemistry. Ti3C2 MXene’s high conductivity can aid in effective charge separation and transfer, thus enhancing photocatalytic efficiency, which is beneficial for applications such as solar energy conversion, environmental cleanup, and hydrogen production [22,23]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) is recognized as a semiconductor possessing a broad bandgap and a significant exciton binding energy. Due to its robust oxidative properties, chemical stability, and non-toxic nature, ZnO is highly esteemed in the realm of photocatalysis. These characteristics render ZnO as an effective photocatalyst for degradation of organic contaminants, water splitting to produce hydrogen, and other environmental and energy-related tasks. The ability of ZnO to create electron-hole pairs when exposed to ultraviolet light enables it to efficiently propel various photocatalytic processes, establishing ZnO as a pivotal material in endeavors aimed at sustainable energy generation and pollution mitigation [9,24,25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale fragments of graphene with unique electronic and optical properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects. They exhibit strong photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, and low toxicity, making them highly suitable for photocatalysis. GQDs can act as effective photosensitizers, enhancing the absorption of visible light and promoting the generation of reactive oxygen species. These features enable GQDs to improve efficiencies of photocatalytic processes such as pollutant degradation, water splitting, and solar energy conversion, contributing to advancements in environmental remediation and renewable energy technologies [26,27].Scheme 1

In this work, Ti3C2 MXene produced through HF etching was further treated to combine it with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and zinc oxide (ZnO). This work investigated electron cloud distribution on the surface of few-layered Ti3C2 MXene and a photoexcited pharmaceutical decomposition process. Effects of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composites in decomposing a wide range of antibiotics over the entire spectrum range were investigated (Table 1 ).

Ti-metal powder (99 %), Al-metal powder (99 %), graphite powder (99 %), Cadmium Selenide powder (99 %), Zinc Oxide (99 %) and hydrofluoric acid (49 %) were ordered from Duksan Science, South Korea (All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade).

Materials were examined using an XRD apparatus (SHIMADZU XRD-6000, Japan) that had a Cu Ka X-ray source with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. Chemical compositions were determined using Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis along with a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) system (SEM-EDX; JSM-5600 JEOL, Japan). Morphologies of samples were analysed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The TEM used was a Hitachi HT7700 operated at 100 kV and the HRTEM used was a Hitachi H9500 operated at 300 kV. A UV–vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2600, Japan) was used to acquire diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Bandgap energies of photocatalysts were determined using a modified Kubelka-Munk function to UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) data. The resistivity of each sample was determined using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with an electrochemical analyzer (Zahner, Germany). Photocurrent measurements were performed using an SLS 301-stabilized tabletop Tungsten-Halogen light source equipped with a 150 W Halogen lamp. The light source covered a wavelength range from 360 nm to 2700 nm. It had an output optical power of nearly 1.6 W. A Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer, Fliorolog-QM from Horiba, operated at a wavelength of 635 nm with a tolerance of ±10 nm. The device consisted of a pico-second pulsed diode laser and a highly efficient, environmentally friendly continuous 75 W Xenon arc lamp with the option to upgrade to a 450 W Xenon lamp.

5 grams of graphite oxide were combined with 5 % NaOH and synthesized via hydrothermal methods at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the mixture was washed many times with deionized water and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 24 h [35,77,78]

The MXene compound was produced through the treatment of the Ti3AlC2 MAX phase with hydrofluoric acid (HF). The MAX phase was synthesised through the combination of pure titanium, aluminium, and graphite in a ratio of 3:1:2. The amalgamation was deposited into an aluminium crucible. The crucible was synthesis to a temperature of 1200 °C for a duration of 2 h. After completing the process of cooling down to the ambient room temperature, which is around 25 °C. The material was subsequently purified by washing it with ultrasonicated distilled water. To synthesize Ti3C2 MXene, the Ti3AlC2 MAX compound was combined with a 49 % hydrofluoric acid (HF) solution at a ratio of 1 mL of HF per 0.1 g of MAX. The mixture was then agitated continuously for a duration of 36 h. The etching process resulted in the removal of the aluminium layer, leading to the exfoliation of the Ti3AlC2 MAX material. The Ti3AlC2 MAX was incrementally introduced into the HF solution to account for the exothermic characteristics of the exfoliation reaction. The resultant MXene was extensively rinsed with deionized water until the solution achieved a pH of 6. Afterwards, the substance was gathered through the process of centrifugation and subjected to drying at a temperature of 60 °C for a duration of 12 h. [23,35]. After finish synthesis the Ti3C2 MXene, 1 g of Zinc Oxide (ZnO), 1 g Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), and 1 g Ti3C2 MXene was added in the Hydrothermal Teflon, and put in the oven 180 °C for 12 h, after finish, dry in the oven 24 h to make it become dry, and the powder keep in the desiccator.

Add the 50 mg of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO to various pollutant solutions (10 ppm) with a final volume of 200 mL, the sample was mixed in a dark room for 60 min to reach equilibrium. Following this, visible light (75 W) was turned on. A 5 mL sample was collected after various mixing times and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. Once the optimal conditions for photodegradation were identified, the same preparation conditions were used to compare degradation by magnetocatalyst, and PMC. The concentration of each sample was analyzed using the UV–vis spectrophotometric method (Uv–Vis Spectro Optizen POP) at wavelengths 614 nm for Amoxicillin [16] and 221 nm for Ibuprofen [36]. Photodegradation efficiency (%) was calculated using the formula (1 − C/C0) × 100, where C0 is the initial pollutant concentration in the solution and C is the pollutant concentration after a specific period of irradiation. Photocatalysis can degrade pollutants following a first-order kinetic model, expressed as ln (C0/C) = k1t, where k1 is the rate constant for a first-order reaction. Previous studies have examined the effects of various experimental factors, such as initial solution pH, photocatalyst loading, and initial pollutant concentration, on the photoactivities of pollutants.

A film was produced using a traditional doctor-blade technique, in which the film was coated with Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO. The enhanced doctor-blade technique facilitated meticulous regulation of the thickness of the resultant powder. Initially, a quantity of 1.1 grammes of synthetic fabric powder was combined with ethyl cellulose and acetone, measuring 1.5 ml, in a mortar. The mixture was stirred for a period of 15 min. Subsequently, the adhesive that was generated was applied onto FTO glass to form a slender coating, which was subsequently allowed to air-dry in an unobstructed space for a duration of 35 min. In order to prevent fractures, a single droplet of lubricating oil was applied to the surface of the film and kept at a consistent temperature of 374 K in a dry oven for 25 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and morphology of catalyst powder
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO was confirmed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Results are displayed in Fig. 1 (a). The ZnO crystal exhibited distinct 2-theta values at 31.73 (100), 34.5 (002), 36.2 (101), and 56.5 (110) according to JCPDs (PDF#36-1451) [49]. Diffraction peaks of Ti3C2 Mxene obtained by treating the precursor MAX with HF were precisely correlated with typical XRD peaks of Ti3C2 MXene. The Ti3C2 MXene exhibited peaks at 2-theta values of 9.6° (002), 43.3° (105), and 62.5° (110) according to the JCPDs database (PDF#032-1383) [50]. GQDs displayed distinct peaks at around 26.4° (002). Properties of Ti3C2MXene/GQDs@ZnO were examined using SEM and TEM. Fig. 1(b) displays the SEM image of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO with EDX analysis. The EDX analysis revealed the presence of carbon (40.28 %), oxygen (20.16 %), titanium (38.46 %), and zinc (1.1 %) in atomic percentages. Fig. 1(c) illustrates element mapping of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO. Fig. S1 illustrates characterization of GQDs, Ti3C2 Mxene, and Ti3C2 Mxene/GQDs. The HR-TEM technique was used to further investigate the intricate morphology and internal structure of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 1(d), HR-TEM analysis provided additional evidence of the presence of ZnO and GQDs across the Ti3C2 MXene. This figure clearly demonstrates that Ti3AlC2 is successfully exfoliated into Ti3C2 MXene with only a few layers. Fig. S2 displays TEM images of GQDs, Ti3C2 MXene, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) study was performed to provide a comprehensive understanding of the surface chemistry of MXene. The elemental composition of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO is depicted in Fig. 2 . Fig. S3 displays XPS survey full spectra, highlighting peaks of Zn2p, O1s, Ti2p, and C1s. Fig. 2(a) demonstrates the presence of doublet spectral lines of Zn2p at binding energies of 1021.7 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1044.8 eV (Zn 2p1/2). These results aligned with findings for Zn2+ in ZnO [42,43]. Fig. 2(b) presents high-resolution spectra of O1s, revealing two prominent peaks at 531.0 and 531.9 eV, which corresponded to C-O-C binding and C-O bonding, respectively [37,38]. Fig. 2(c) shows deconvolution of Ti2p, Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 (463.4 and 457.9 eV) in MXene, revealing that titanium had a fixed area ratio close to 2:1 [39,40]. Fig. 2(d) shows C1s spectrum with two clearly identifiable peaks, one at 283.3 eV corresponding to C–C/–CH bonds and one at 284.2 eV corresponding to C = C bonds [41]. Fig. 2(e) shows deconvolution of Zn2p, displaying measured XPS spectra of the Zn 2p core-level.

Fig. 3 (a) illustrates band gap values estimated from diffuse reflectance using the Kubelka-Munk function. Fig. 3(a) presents diffuse reflectance spectra of different catalyst powders, including GQDs, Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO with band gap energies of around 2.79, 2.82, 2.76, and 2.75 eV, respectively. The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO demonstrated the lowest band gap energy, which had a substantial impact on the photocatalytic result. A photocatalyst with a low bandgap energy has the advantage of being able to absorb a wider range of sunlight, including visible light. The enhanced absorption leads to enhanced effectiveness of the photocatalyst in harnessing solar energy to facilitate chemical activities such as water splitting and pollutant degradation. The photocatalyst can enhance its production of electron-hole pairs by catching additional sunlight, which is essential for beginning and maintaining these reactions. A decrease in bandgap energy results in an increase in photo-catalytic activity, hence enhancing the effectiveness and efficiency of the process for applications in renewable energy and environmental remediation [44–46]. Fig. 3(b) displays Mott-Schottky plots of several catalysts in the form of powder. Mott-Schottky (M–S) test is a useful method for examining the conduction type, flat band potential (Vfb), and estimated main charge carrier densities of a catalyst powder. Mott-Schottky plots were generated for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO by depositing them at different potentials. Nanorod arrays had positive curvature slopes, validating their n-type semiconductor characteristics. These nanorod arrays deposited on Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO had Vfb values of 0.75, 0.61, and 0.37 V, respectively. The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO material exhibited an excellent photocatalytic performance in generating hydrogen in photoelectrochemical cells, as evidenced by its significantly lower Efb value of 0.37 V. The low rate of electron/hole recombination and the high electron transition observed in the structure can be ascribed to this phenomenon [47,48].

Photoluminescence spectra of different catalyst powders were compared. Results are displayed in Fig. 3(c). Photoluminescence spectra of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO showed a diminished intensity in comparison with those of Ti3C2 MXene and the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs. The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composite is expected to exhibit excellent photocatalytic properties due to its low photoluminescence intensity. A low intensity can reduce recombination of electron-hole pairs, leading to a higher concentration of charge carriers available for participation in photocatalytic events by increasing the likelihood of these charges triggering chemical processes such as degradation of pollutants and production of hydrogen, thus enhancing the effectiveness of the photocatalyst [51,52]. PL spectra results provided support for findings of Kubelka-Munk and Mott-Schottky calculations in photocatalysts, indicating that the reduction of electron-hole recombination and increased light absorption collectively boosted the efficiency of photocatalysis. The efficiency of a photocatalyst is greatly influenced by the decay time, which quantifies the duration for which excited electron-hole pairs persist before recombining. A longer decay time implies that charge carriers have a greater duration to engage in photocatalytic processes, hence amplifying the overall activity and efficacy of a photocatalyst in applications such as pollutant degradation and hydrogen production [53]. Fig. 3(d) shows results of decay time analysis of various catalyst powder. It was revealed that Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO had a higher intensity than other catalyst powders such as Ti3C2 MXene and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs. It indicates that excited electron-hole pairs persist longer before recombination. Synergistic utilization of ZnO, Ti3C2 MXene, and GQDs can lead to a significant improvement in decay time by facilitating efficient separation of charge carriers. ZnO known for its effectiveness as a photocatalyst normally suffers from rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs. By incorporating Ti3C2 MXene and GQDs, Ti3C2 MXenes exhibited a remarkable electrical conductivity. They could function as electron sinks, efficiently trapping electrons from ZnO. The separation of electrons and holes in GQDs inhibited their rapid recombination, while also endowed GQDs with robust electron-accepting and electron-transporting capabilities. Additionally, they could collect and transmit electrons, hence increasing the separation of charge carriers. The decay duration of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composite was greatly enhanced. Consequently, photo-generated electrons and holes persist edin a state of separation for an extended period, thereby augmenting the overall efficiency of the photocatalytic process [54–57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative electrochemical analysis of catalyst powder
ce:section.ce:para: Transient photocurrent primarily arises from separation and diffusion of photogenerated electrons and holes inside the internal structure of a photocatalyst, leading to their migration towards free charged acceptors present on the surface of the photocatalyst and in the electrolyte. This study utilized photoelectric chemical measurements to examine the efficiency of separation and the kinetics of transfer of photo-induced electron-hole pairs in the Z-scheme Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO heterojunction system. Transient photocurrent responses of Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO samples were measured under visible light conditions, with the light either turned on or off as depicted in Fig. 4 a. The photocurrent intensity of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO was significantly greater than that of pure Ti3C2 MXene or Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, indicating that the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst possesses optimal charge separation, enhanced reactivity, and accelerated electron transport, all of which contribute to superior photocatalytic performance. The catalyst powder's quantum efficiency (η) was established by examining measured photocurrent responses. Fig. 4(b) displays quantum efficiency (η), which represents the ratio of electron-hole carrier pairs produced per absorbed photon of energy hυ. The value of η is determined suing the following equation: η = (number of electron-hole pairs generated)/(number of incident-absorbed photons) = (Ip × hυ)/(q × Pin), where Ip is the photocurrent generated by a steady-state optical power and Pin is the incident power on the photoreactor (1.6 W). Over time, the quantum efficiency (η) of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO electrodes showed a notable and continuous improvement. The optimum quantum efficiency refers to the favorable impact it has on the photocatalyst as it enables a higher percentage of absorbed photons to be efficiently employed for chemical reactions. This enhances both the reaction rate and energy efficiency [58].

Electrochemical characteristics of Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO electrodes were examined using cyclic voltammetry (CV) as depicted in Fig. 4(c). The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composite exhibited more pronounced anodic and cathodic peaks than other catalyst powders. The inclusion of MXene increased the specific surface area of the working electrode as a result of formation of several layers of MXene. By incorporating GQD and ZnO onto the surface of Ti3C2 MXene, both electrochemical conductivity and surface area of the electrode were improved, resulting in an elevated specific surface area higher cyclic voltammetry current for catalyst powders reflects increased surface area and active sites, improved charge transfer kinetics, and enhanced electrochemical stability, all of which contribute to superior photocatalytic performance. The presence of amoxicillin in Fig. 5 (a) and ibuprofen in Fig. 5(b) resulted in a reduction in the surface area under the CV test. This was due to adsorption of amoxicillin and Ibuprofen onto the electrode surface, which formed a passivating layer. This passivating layer decreased the number of active sites available for electrochemical reactions, leading to a smaller surface area in CV tests. Moreover, these compounds have the potential to disrupt the electrolyte or electroactive substances, leading to changes in electrochemical conditions and a subsequent reduction in the measured surface area [59–61]. Fig. 4(d) shows Nyquist plots of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO electrodes with and without visible light irradiation. The radius of the arc in the EIS spectra corresponds to the charge separation and transfer resistance at the electrode. A smaller radius indicates a higher charge separation efficiency. The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composite exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance. The outcome affirmed that the layered Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO, which possessed excellent conductivity and active sites, enabled rapid charge transfer, leading to a highly effective photocatalytic activity. This enhanced performance was attributed to the strong and uniform interfacial connection, which greatly enhanced the charge transfer behavior. Effects of amoxicillin and ibuprofen on the EIS test were demonstrated in Fig. 5(e) and (f), illustrating that these medicatTransient photocurrent response of catalyst powder ions enhanced the charge transfer. This effect was attributed to their ability to increase conductivity in the solution and facilitate electron transfer processes during electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests. Their existence could potentially alter characteristics of the electrode surface, resulting in a decrease in charge transfer resistance and facilitating more effective electron mobility [62–64]. In this study, the Tafel test was used to analyze the kinetics of electron transfer processes and assess the efficiency of the photocatalyst. Tafel test results are shown in Fig. 4(e). Results revealed that Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO had the least amount of corrosion density. This low corrosion current density of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO indicates that this photocatalyst is highly resistant to degradation. Thus, it can maintain its structural integrity over time. The stability of a photocatalyst during applications is essential for long-term efficiency and reliability, hence minimizing the requirement for regular replacements or maintenance [65]. Effects of amoxicillin and ibuprofen on corrosion density were determined using Tafel tests. Results are shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). Amoxicillin and ibuprofen increased the corrosion density in the Tafel test by reacting with the electrode surface and altering its properties. These interactions might potentially exacerbate corrosive conditions or amplify the electrochemical activity, resulting in higher corrosion rates [66,67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various methods (photocatalyst, magnetocatalyst, and PMC) for degradation pollutants amoxicillin and ibuprofen
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation of Amoxicillin and Ibuprofen was studied using different catalyst powders, namely Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO. After determining the most effective catalyst powder for visible light photodegradation, various degradation methods including photocatalysis, magnetocatalysis, and PMC were analyzed. The optimization of three factors, namely pH, initial pollutant concentration, and initial catalyst powder concentration, was carried out. Subsequently, the impact of scavengers, as well as the potential for reusability and durability of the catalyst powder, were examined under ideal circumstances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the condition of catalyst powder using photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic efficiencies of the produced Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO were assessed by studying the breakdown of amoxicillin and ibuprofen in an aqueous solution under visible light exposure. Initially, amoxicillin adsorption was assessed under non-irradiated conditions (in the absence of light) for a duration of 60 min. Results are shown in Fig. 6 (a), (c) and (e). Significant adsorption ranging from 10 % to 60 % was found for all catalyst materials. Various capacities for adsorption were anticipated because of their distinct yet comparable BET surface areas. Subsequently, the process of amoxicillin photodegradation was assessed over a duration of 240 min in the presence of white light. Overall, all three catalyst materials exhibited a superior amoxicillin photodegradation profile to a standard amoxicillin reaction (sample containing simply amoxicillin solution exposed to visible light). The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst underwent a degradation of 99 % after 240 min with K = 0.03 min−1 and R2 = 0.98. After 240 min of the reaction, it was discovered that the powder of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO exhibited a 22 % elimination of amoxicillin in the dark. The degradation of ibuprofen by Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO during photodegradation is illustrated in Fig. 6(b), (d), and (f). After 240 min of photodegradation under visible light, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO reduced the concentration of ibuprofen the most, achieving a decrease of 93 % when compared to other catalyst powders (K = 0.07 min−1, R2 of 0.98). Furthermore, the performance of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst was assessed using several degradation methods to determine the most effective approach for breaking down amoxicillin and ibuprofen. Results revealed that the PMC outperformed both the photocatalyst and magnetic catalyst. According to Fig. 7 (a) and (b), the degradation of amoxicillin using PMC reached its optimal state with a decrease of 99 % at a rate of K = 0.064 min−1 and a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.99 after 80 min. Its degradation ability was better than the degradation achieved using the photocatalyst or the magnetic catalyst. The degradation of ibuprofen was also investigated using different methods. Results are illustrated in Fig. 7(c) and (d). Results demonstrated that Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst with PMC resulted in a 97 % decrease in ibuprofen concentration, betther than other catalysts. Its degradation rate constant (K) was determined to be 0.069 min−1, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99). This significant reduction in ibuprofen concentration was achieved after 240 min of reaction time, surpassing performances of both the photocatalyst and the magnetic catalyst.

Furthermore, the performance of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst was assessed using various concentrations of amoxicillin and ibuprofen to determine its maximum adsorption capacity. Results indicate that this material exhibited superior degradation activity for amoxicillin and ibuprofen solutions at concentrations up to 5 ppm, as depicted in Fig. 8 (a) and (b). With magnetic photocatalytic systems, higher concentrations of pollutants reduced degradation percentages by creating a thick layer that hindered the movement of reactants to the surface of the photocatalyst and limited the penetration of light. This diminished the effectiveness of both magnetic and photocatalytic processes, resulting in decreased rates of degradation [68]. This study investigated effects of different concentrations (25, 50, and 75 mg) of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst on the degradation of amoxicillin and ibuprofen utilizing magnetophotocatalytic methods. The degradation of amoxicillin and ibuprofen was optimal in the presence of a higher amount (75 mg) of the catalyst powder Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO, as depicted in Fig. 8(c) and (d). A higher amount of the catalyst powder increased the percentage of pollutant degradation due to the presence of more active sites for photocatalytic reactions, hence boosting the efficiency of degradation. Moreover, an increase of the catalyst surface area can enhance the interaction between the reactant and the catalyst, resulting in an elevated %degradation [68,69]. The pH is a crucial factor as it has a substantial impact on the surface charge of a photocatalyst during the degradation of pollutants by a magnetic photocatalyst. After establishing the ideal conditions in the previous studied, the impact of pH value on the efficiency of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO as a magnetic photocatalyst for removing Amoxicillin and Ibuprofen. The findings are presented in Fig. 8(e) and (f). Based on the previous experiment results, the study was conducted using a degradation time of 240 min to investigate the impact of pH values ranging from 3 to 11. The result shown that the Ideal conditions the pH level for the degradation of Amoxicillin is the ionisation state of amoxicillin at pH 5 facilitates its interaction with the photocatalyst surface, thereby enhancing efficient adsorption and degradation. Furthermore, a pH 5 promotes the production of ROS for photodegradation of Amoxicillin, such as superoxide radicals, which play a vital role in the degradation of amoxicillin molecules [70,71] and the optimum condition pH for degradation of Ibuprofen is pH 7, In photodegradation of ibuprofen at pH 7, the photocatalyst absorbs light, generating electron-hole pairs that lead to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) like superoxide radicals (
O2 −). These ROS attack the deprotonated (anionic) form of ibuprofen, breaking down its aromatic ring and side chains through oxidation reactions. The resulting intermediates are further degraded by ROS, eventually mineralizing into CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions [72–74]. The behavior of reactive oxygen species (ROSs) was examined in a quench experiment to learn more about the PMC reaction mechanism. Before the PMC process was conducted under different conditions, four specific quenchers were used: benzoquinone (BQ) to suppress superoxide radicals (
O2−), isopropyl (IPA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to suppress hydroxyl radicals (
OH), and potassium iodide (KI) to suppress holes (h+ quencher). Fig. 8 displays the outcomes of the scavenger effect. Fig. 8(g) shows the %degradation of Amoxicillin has been decrease from 99 % to 38 % after adding the BQ as a scavenger, It has been determined that the presence of O2 − ions significantly contributes to the breakdown of Amoxicillin. This degradation process is facilitated by the use of a catalyst called Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO with a magnetic photocatalyst technique [75]. Fig. 8(f) shows the effect of scavenger on the degradation of ibuprofen using Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO. The %degradation of ibuprofen was decreased from 97 % to 28 % after adding BQ. This shows that O2 − ions have a major effect on the degradation process [76,77].

After determining the optimal conditions for pollutant degradation by the magnetic photocatalyst, the impact of voltage was investigated due to its potential to affect both alignment and strength of the magnetic field. Multiple voltage magnetic configurations were investigated. Results are depicted in Fig. 9 . The degradation of amoxicillin reached 99 % after 40 min under optimal conditions of 10.8 voltages, with a degradation K = 0.03 min−1 and R2 = 0.98 as depicted in Fig. 9(a) and (c). Similarly, the degradation of ibuprofen under various voltage conditions showed that the optimal condition for degradation was 10.8 voltages (K = 0.003 min−1, R2 = 0.99), resulting in a 99 % degradation after 240 min as shown in Fig. 9(b) and (d).

To improve our comprehension of PMC, it is crucial to investigating boundaries of the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of the components included in the composite. Conceptually, a semiconductor catalyst has the ability to generate electrons (e−) and holes (h+) in its conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) when it absorbs photon energy. Results of assessing the potential of CB and VB edges of MXene/GQDs@ZnO are presented in Table 2 and Fig. 10 . Table 2 demonstrates the application of this method in identifying possible locations for H2O/O2 and understanding the process of charge transfer in Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO nanocomposites. The normal redox potential of H2O/O2 reduction is 1.23 V compared to the conventional hydrogen electrode (NHE). The hydroxyl radical (OH
) exhibits a standard redox potential of 2.3 V compared to the usual hydrogen electrode (NHE) when water (H2O) is present. Table 2 displays electrochemical potentials of Ti3C2 Mxene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO compared to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). Electrochemical potentials (Ecb/V) of Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO were measured to be 3.017, 2.957, and 2.947 V, respectively, relative to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). Electrochemical potentials (Ecb/V) for Ti3C2 MXene, Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs, and Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO were 0.966, 0.826, and 0.586 V, respectively, compared to a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE). Equations provided below outline active oxidation compounds generated during the magnetic photocatalyst process:
(1) Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO + hν → e− + h+
(2) h+ + H2O → H+ +
OH
(3) O2 + e− → O2 −
(4) O2 − + 2H+ → HO2
(5) H2O + )))) →
OH + H
(6) H2O + H
→
OH + H2
(7) Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO/CdSe + )))) → Ti3C2 MXene/ZnO/CdSe (h+ + e−)
(8) Amoxicillin/Ibuprofen + h+/
OH/O2 − → degradation products

This research also investigated the stability of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO during the Photocurrent method. The powder was dried in an oven at 75 °C for 24 h after going through the PMC process once. It was then repeatedly cleaned with DI water. Subsequently, the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO powder was reused and employed for a total of five iterations. Fig. 11 (a) displays the powder Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO photocurrent characteristics after being used 5 times for degrading different pollutants. The results indicate that the performance of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO remained stable. Fig. 11(b) demonstrates that the % PMC of the Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO catalyst powder remained unchanged compared to the first time it was used, Additionally, Fig. 11(c) shows the characteristic before-after PMC various pollutants, and it’s shown the characteristic of crystal Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO is stable after 5 cycle’s, respectively.

A novel Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO photocatalyst was successfully synthesized using a simple self-assembly method. The catalyst's physical and chemical properties were thoroughly characterized using advanced techniques such as XRD, FESEM, TEM, XPS, and DRS. The findings revealed that the addition of a magnetic field significantly enhanced the photocatalytic degradation efficiency for pharmaceutical contaminants, specifically amoxicillin and ibuprofen. The Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO composite achieved high degradation efficiencies of 99 % for amoxicillin and 97 % for ibuprofen under optimal photomagnetocatalytic (PMC) conditions, demonstrating the optimum performance of this composite compared to conventional photocatalytic or magnetic catalysts. The catalyst exhibited excellent stability and reusability, maintaining degradation efficiency over five reuse cycles. Additionally, the scavenging experiments confirmed the dominant role of superoxide radicals (
O2 −) in the degradation process. These results highlight the potential of Ti3C2 MXene/GQDs@ZnO in the effective treatment of pharmaceutical pollutants in wastewater, suggesting its suitability for large-scale environmental applications. Therefore, future work could focus on optimizing the system for industrial-scale water treatment, exploring the degradation of other antibiotic classes or investigating the scalability of the process and further exploring the synergistic effects of the magnetic field and photocatalysis in pollutant degradation to answer the limitation of this study.

David Nugroho: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Young Jun Joo: Formal analysis, Data curation. Kwang Youn Cho: Methodology, Formal analysis. Rachadaporn Benchawattananon: Investigation. Saksit Chanthai: Methodology, Investigation. Won-Chun Oh: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.
